step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("Glomerulonephritis conditions causing nephrotic syndrome")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

To further refine my decision:
Post-infectious glomerulonephritis (PSGN) indeed minimizes the chances of developing nephrotic syndrome compared to other listed conditions. While there's no strict prohibition against it causing nephrotic syndrome, historically and generally speaking, PSGN predominantly exhibits signs consistent with a nephritic rather than purely nephrotic picture. Other choices - minimal change disease, FSGS, and MPGN - are strongly correlated with classic nephrotic syndrome presentations due to direct structural changes or inherent mechanisms disrupting normal filtration barrier integrity without substantial cellular infiltration seen in PSGN.
Based on the above review of detailed evidences highlighting prevalence rates and characteristics of each condition, I reaffirm my agreement with earlier conclusions:
\[ \boxed{\text{B}} \] \boxed{{B}}
This reiteration supports the initial stance considering epidemiological trends supporting minimal chance of PSGN being solely responsible for nephrotic syndrome among provided options.
